original_hypothesis.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Inflate Escrow Tracked Balance Causing Settlement Failures
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: PaymentEscrow.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the order, but fee-on-transfer ERC20 tokens deliver less than the specified amount. This inflates balanceOf[token] relative to the actual token balance. When settlePayment attempts to transfer the full pre-fee amount, it may fail due to insufficient actual balance, permanently locking lender's NFTs because stopRent cannot complete.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: increaseDeposit adds the full item.amount to balanceOf[token]. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the actual received amount is less. After multiple orders, the tracked balance exceeds the real balance. During settlement, _safeTransfer attempts to transfer amounts that don't exist, reverting the entire stopRent transaction.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol signer does not filter fee-on-transfer tokens when signing rental payloads
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Documentation states FOT tokens are not supported but no on-chain enforcement exists in Create.sol or PaymentEscrow.sol

proof_of_signal.pattern: _increaseDeposit(token, amount) records nominal amount; no check that actual received matches

preconditions: Protocol signer signs a rental payload using a fee-on-transfer ERC20
The token charges a transfer fee reducing actual amount received by escrow
A subsequent rental stop triggers settlement

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Escrow tracked balance diverges from actual balance after FOT token deposits
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Protocol signer must authorize FOT token rental (off-chain)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After any FOT token rental creation

operational_questions: Does the protocol signer have a token allowlist that excludes FOT tokens?
Can skim() recover from negative delta (trueBalance < syncedBalance)?
Would the overflow in skim() permanently DoS all operations for that token?
validation_requirements: Create rental with FOT token, verify balanceOf[token] > actual balance
Attempt to stop the rental and observe settlement revert
Test skim() behavior when trueBalance < syncedBalance (expected underflow revert)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Request protocol signer to authorize a rental with a fee-on-transfer ERC20 payment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Fulfill the order — escrow records full amount but receives less
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When rental expires, stopRent attempts settlement but _safeTransfer fails
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lender's NFTs permanently locked in rental safe; escrow balance corrupted
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires protocol signer to not filter the token

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Affected rentals permanently unstoppable; lender NFTs locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manual intervention or contract upgrade to fix accounting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rentals and settlements involving the FOT token

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low - standard rental creation with FOT token

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rental creation with known fee-on-transfer tokens

validation_result.mechanism: FOT token accounting mismatch in escrow
validation_result.impact_summary: Fee-on-transfer tokens cause PaymentEscrow.balanceOf[token] to exceed the actual token balance, leading to settlement failures that permanently lock lender NFTs in rental safes.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that PaymentEscrow.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the Seaport order without verifying the actual amount received. For fee-on-transfer (FOT) tokens, the actual received amount is less than the nominal amount because the token contract deducts a fee during transfer. This creates a persistent accounting mismatch where balanceOf[token] > IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(escrow)). During settlement, _settlePayment calls _decreaseDeposit with the full nominal amount (which succeeds because it only decrements the tracked balance) and then attempts _safeTransfer for the payment amounts. If the actual token balance is insufficient, the transfer reverts, making stopRent fail. Additionally, the skim() function computes trueBalance - syncedBalance which would underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8.x) when trueBalance < syncedBalance, making it impossible to recover from this state without a contract upgrade.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly present in the code. increaseDeposit blindly adds the nominal amount to balanceOf[token] without any balance-before/balance-after check. The _settlePayment function then attempts to transfer amounts based on the inflated tracked balance. There is no on-chain validation that the token is not a FOT token in either Create.sol or PaymentEscrow.sol.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Records the nominal amount without checking actual tokens received. For FOT tokens, the escrow receives less than 'amount', inflating balanceOf[token] above the actual balance.
No balance-before/balance-after check. Simply forwards the nominal amount to _increaseDeposit regardless of actual tokens received.
Passes the nominal order amount directly to increaseDeposit without accounting for any transfer fees the token may charge.
When trueBalance < syncedBalance (which occurs with FOT tokens), this subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8.x, reverting the transaction. This makes skim permanently DoS'd for affected tokens.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
_decreaseDeposit(item.token, item.amount);
// ... then calls _safeTransfer for lender/renter amounts
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Attempts to transfer amounts based on the inflated tracked balance. If actual balance is insufficient, _safeTransfer reverts, blocking the entire stopRent transaction.

calling_sequence: 1. Seaport calls Create.validateOrder() after fulfilling order with FOT token payment
3. _rentFromZone processes items and calls ESCRW.increaseDeposit(token, item.amount) for each ERC20
4. increaseDeposit calls _increaseDeposit which does balanceOf[token] += amount (nominal amount)
5. At this point, balanceOf[token] > IERC20(token).balanceOf(escrow) because FOT fee was deducted during transfer
6. Later, Stop.stopRent() is called, which calls ESCRW.settlePayment(order)
8. _safeTransfer attempts to transfer more tokens than actually exist, causing revert
9. stopRent reverts entirely, lender's NFTs remain locked in rental safe
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability path starts at Create.validateOrder where Seaport reports the nominal transfer amount. This amount flows through _rentFromZone to ESCRW.increaseDeposit, which naively records it. The actual token transfer (handled by Seaport) delivers fewer tokens due to the FOT fee. During stopRent -> settlePayment -> _settlePayment, the code attempts to transfer amounts derived from the inflated tracked balance. When the cumulative transfers exceed actual balance, _safeTransfer reverts, making the rental unstoppable.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Protocol signer does not filter fee-on-transfer tokens when signing rental payloads
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployProtocol.s.sol grants CREATE_SIGNER role to config.serverSideSigner() but contains no token filtering logic. No allowlist or blocklist for tokens is visible in any deployment script. The serverSideSigner configuration is external to the provided code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid: (1) A rental is created using a FOT token as payment. Seaport transfers tokens to the escrow, but the FOT mechanism deducts a fee. (2) increaseDeposit records the full nominal amount, creating balanceOf[token] > actual balance. (3) When stopRent is called, _settlePayment attempts to transfer more tokens than the escrow actually holds. (4) The _safeTransfer call reverts because the ERC20 transfer fails. (5) The entire stopRent transaction reverts. (6) The lender's NFTs remain locked in the rental safe indefinitely. The attack requires the protocol signer to authorize the FOT token, which is an off-chain operational dependency with no on-chain enforcement.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. If a FOT token is used in a rental: (1) The specific rental becomes permanently unstoppable, locking lender NFTs. (2) The skim() function for that token permanently reverts due to underflow. (3) If multiple rentals use the same FOT token, earlier rentals may settle but later ones will fail as the cumulative deficit grows. (4) Recovery requires a contract upgrade of the PaymentEscrow module. The defect is a missing accounting safeguard - the code should measure actual received amounts rather than trusting nominal amounts.
recommended_mitigations: Add balance-before/balance-after check in increaseDeposit: record IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) before and after the deposit, and use the difference as the actual deposited amount.
Add an on-chain token whitelist that Create.validateOrder checks before processing ERC20 payment items.
Consider adding a check in skim() that handles the case where trueBalance < syncedBalance gracefully instead of reverting.
Document clearly that FOT tokens are unsupported and add on-chain enforcement rather than relying solely on off-chain signer policy.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy protocol with standard setup. 2. Deploy a mock FOT ERC20 token with a 5% transfer fee. 3. Create a BASE rental order with the FOT token as payment. 4. Verify that after order creation, PaymentEscrow.balanceOf[fotToken] > IERC20(fotToken).balanceOf(escrow). 5. Wait for rental to expire. 6. Call stopRent and observe that it reverts during _safeTransfer. 7. Verify that skim(fotToken, ...) also reverts due to underflow. 8. Confirm that lender's NFT remains locked in the rental safe.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol signer's off-chain implementation has a token allowlist that effectively prevents FOT tokens from being used (this is not visible in the provided code or deployment scripts).
Whether Seaport itself has any handling for FOT tokens that might affect the flow (e.g., if Seaport checks post-transfer balances).
additional_testing_needed: Test with actual FOT token to confirm settlement failure
Test skim() underflow behavior with FOT tokens
Test with multiple rentals using the same FOT token to verify cascading failures
Test whether Seaport's execution invariants catch the discrepancy before reaching Create.validateOrder
